Let's see, hey, I'm on your page 42. Don't need to spend a lot of time. It's basically obvious once you think about it. It is that language matters, and euphemisms are powerful tools. Almost all society will change. Revolves around invention of palatable euphemisms for what is unpalatable when it is stated boldly for what it is. So the example, estate tax versus death tax, right? Death tax is unpalatable to almost everybody. So even poor people who would like to tax rich people and who are philosophically opposed to dynastic movements of money from one generation to another, basically recoil at the idea of a death tax. Because it makes it sound like, right with your last rattles, there's a tax collector standing there by the bed and you have to pay to leave. That's kind of what it makes it sound like, right? Whereas estate tax, first of all, most people don't have estates. They don't even think about that term, right? They think rich people have estates. And when they hear a state more often than not, they think about a mansion on big grounds with a castle on the hill and a Rolls Royce parked in front of the fountain. And well, tax that all you want. Go get them, right? That's their reaction to that. Death tax, nobody likes that. Even people who would be philosophically in favor of estate taxes on the rich. So language, right? So we have made, depending on where you are, what your position is on this. So, you know, look at the illegal issue. See, undocumented sounds less offensive than illegal alien. Illegal alien sounds less offensive than criminal alien. And it allows, Hillary's constant argument, most of these people are not criminals. Trump accused them all of being criminals, which he didn't. Most of these people are not criminals. Well, by definition they are, regardless of what you think about the issue. They broke in. If somebody breaks into your house, even if they don't take anything, they break in and when you come home tonight, they're sitting on the couch, watching TV. They're a criminal. By definition, they broke in. There's no question. And if we really, if you want to win this issue, you should have stubbornly stuck with criminal aliens. That's what you should have stuck with. Minimum wage. So the language that they want, that advocates of higher minimum wages want to use, is living wage. And that's what we're going to wind up with in very short order, by the way. If you listen, this progression has occurred, this is nothing new. It's right out of Alinsky, it's right out of Bernays, propaganda. You've got to use palatable euphemisms and you have to change the language of the thing if you are going to change acceptance of the thing. So minimum wage is becoming living wage. If we wanted to win that argument, we should have fought tooth and nail for entry wage. Starter wage. Even clumsy. First rung on the ladder wage. And when everybody said minimum wage, and now when everybody is saying living wage, if everybody on the other side immediately corrected the reporter, the pundit, whoever, let me be clear before I answer the question, what you mean is the starter wage, the first rung on the ladder wage, is that what we're talking about? That would go a long way towards changing this argument. The argument is being lost through the acceptance of the palatable euphemism. As soon as everybody starts arguing over a living wage, the argument is lost. Because conceptually, nobody wants to deny anybody a living wage. You sound like an absolute asshole if you sit there and argue, no, people should not have a living wage. I mean, how are you going to win that argument? Right? But if you argue it's a starter wage, nobody is supposed to live on it. That's not its purpose. Right? They're supposed to start there. If you go get a job at the convenience store down the street here at BP, and you get the night shift job, which there's a sign, they're hiring, it's like $8 an hour, it's horrible. You're risking your life, you're working 12 to 7, and you only get to eat the stale donuts, and no, you can't live on $8 an hour. Even if you've got two $8 an hour jobs, you can't live on $8 an hour. You can't acknowledge that. If you have no skills, and you go get that job, and you show up, and you show up on time, and you don't steal much, in six months you'll be on the day shift, in 12 months you'll be a store manager, and in 24 months you'll be a regional manager making $48,000 a year. That's how our system works. Almost every really shit job has a path. And that's its purpose, is to give somebody with no skills a place to start and crawl out of this hole. They ain't supposed to stay there and feed three people on $8 an hour. What should happen, instead of raising that wage, is we should round all them people up, beat the shit out of them, in front of their spouses and children, so everybody gets the message, and then we should take them down to the Army recruiting office, and we should sign them up for two years. And then when they come out, we should say, you have six months not to be in a minimum wage job. If you're still there, we're coming and getting you again, we'll go beat the shit out of you again, and then we're going to put you in the Navy, and then we're going to put you in the Marines, and then we're going to put you in the Air Force. By then you will have figured this out. That's how we should really handle it, right? But you definitely can't have that conversation that I just had, if you call the damn thing a living wage. Right? So this is with everything. We have to make things palatable, euphemism. We have to make things acceptable to people that otherwise are unacceptable just because of what they are named, or how they are described. Right? And so you immediately change kind of everything afterwards by making that change. So like in my business, there is probably no worse word left on earth than workbook. You, it just, who wants it? Name anybody, including you, who wants a workbook. And yet you still see people using this term. That's why there's a phasaurus. This is one of the greatest tools ever invented. And clients will sit there and want to play, rename the tune games with me during a $19,000 consulting day. And I'm thinking, really, you want to spend an hour on this? Go home, get a phasaurus. That's what I do. I got five of them. One of them is right on the computer. So for a lot of quick shit, you can just click. Even I do it. It's inside the dictionary. On mine, it's in a little red, little dictionary. You click it, and there's a dictionary, and there's a phasaurus for every word. Perfect. For a lot of purposes, that does it. Oh, okay. That's what we'll call it. Otherwise, I got like five printed ones, different kinds. And so now, while I'm watching the basketball game, I got to find a palatable euphemism for workbook. I got to find something to call a seminar, but a seminar. What are we going to call it? So this starts to change everything behind it. But if you don't change it, everything behind it is heavier lifting. Right? So this is a very powerful thing. Hijack number nine. And we've referenced it as we've gone along a couple of places. So there is a desire to know why. People want to know why. They don't need much of an explanation, but they kind of feel unsatisfied without an explanation. So like on 44, you don't need to guess. However, for the benefit of Bill and Jason, it's B. Because it has a reason why, which instantly makes it more believable, more satisfying. The person can give a little nod. Oh, okay. This must be true because they've added whatever the hell they've added, probiotic enzymes. Right? So when you are going to put something in front of somebody that they might question, it's useful to have the reason why. Virtually every culture, I get myself in trouble with my friend again, virtually every culture. Many, if you go back far enough in time, did it independent of other cultures. So this was not like working off a swipe file. Virtually every culture developed a religion. Hard to find a culture that didn't. And many developed their religions in isolation, independent of anybody else. So it's not like, okay, I like theirs, but I want somebody that flies. So we'll change a couple things. That's what happened late, but not early. So every culture developed a religion. If it was innate, everybody would have developed the same religion. But they didn't. So it's not innate. It's a response to a need and a desire. It's a coping mechanism. Now, yours may happen to be the right one. And if so, congratulations. But understand that the first few savages who decided there was a sun god, which that religion still exists, they did that to explain the sun. Because what everybody does innately want is an explanation. And so for the unexplainable, the explanation, every culture created for itself was a religion. I'm not passing any judgements. So whatever yours is, again, it might be the right one. And there's a few close enough, there might be three or four right ones. I was raised Lutheran. That's Catholic without confession. And we still ate fish on Friday just in case. And by the way, there's a reason I was not welcome in Catechism class. I don't remember the guy's name anymore, but I actually, so a few things in life I actually feel a little badly about because I really did torment the guy. And he had real loose floppy pants. And by the end of every Catechism class, he was jumping up and down, vibrating so annoyed his pants are going up and down his legs. I mean, I drove him nuts. And I kind of feel bad about it. He probably ended his life prematurely. On the other hand, maybe you should thank me. So this is, so you have on your page 45 a control ad with very little change, 30, 35 years. A friend of mine, Mark Kress, made a lot of money. You guys own old Ron Popeels spray hair in a can. This is not that. This actually is derivative of stuff that was and is used in Hollywood. I used to use it for video and photo when I had enough hair for it to stick to because it's it is what it says and you shake it out of a can like a salt shaker. And it's fabric really, but it's made to stick by static cling to hair. So you can cover up a little bald spot with a pretty good. Unlike what they claim, if it rains, you're still screwed. But and the texture is not right. So if she runs her fingers through her hair, she's coming off with some of this in between your fingers. I mean, you know, it's nine bucks. You kind of get what you pay for. But but like for photography, so a lot of photography studios have it. It's sold to them under a different brand name, but they have it. So they can quickly fix you for a photograph. And if you're doing video, it does work, but you got to have enough. It's got to cling to something. Yeah. But what's important about this ad to know is that this is like the fourth generation of the ad. And each time he added more explanation. Because people want to know how this can work. And the explanation of how this works really helps this ad. You have 14 pages in here from other sources starting on page 46. They're essentially an extra gift. We are skipping over them. They are yours to do with as you wish. And I'm going to page 59. Copywriting hijacked number 10 is specificity. If I do a copy critique, more often than not, this is one of the things I get to critique. And I get to say here's a basher missing. Because most people don't put in enough specifics about the product or service, about the story they are telling, about the testimonial person they are showing off, about anything and everything. So for example, in Miracle Air, we did a lot better once I created specifics about every testimony. So okay, she's a registered nurse. Great. Because that's high credibility. In case you don't know alternative health stuff. RNs are more useful than RMDs. They are more believed. They are more trusted. But how long has she been in RN? Is she at a brand name hospital? Does she have grandkids? Does she, what now? What can we say about this person and their specifics that makes them even more meaningful as a testimonial than, now where I started was name and initial. That's where I started, because that's what they were doing. So that has vague as you can get. Barbara N. It also has incredible as you can get. Because everybody knows we could just be making them up. Two thirds of the time when you see that, they are just making them up. And when, you remember Tommy Vu? Get rich in real estate. SNL used to make fun of the infomercial in the hot tub with the babes. So Tommy's whole infomercial, when he got busted, Tommy went to jail. The entire infomercial was shot word for word from another get rich in real estate guru show from the 1960s. So not only didn't he change the script, which would be bad enough, he didn't change the testimonials. Here you just had the testimonials say the same thing that the testimonials said in that. Well, you know, why reinvent the wheel? The regulators noticed. And it was hard to make an argument than that any of this might be real. But generally when you see Nancy R, often there is no Nancy R. And even if there is, people are going to think there's no Nancy R. And so the more you make that person real, the better you are. The more story details. Right? If it's on a dark and stormy night, that's better than just starting with I was driving down a road. How does that help sell something? It does because it makes the story believable. It's the trivial details that make these things work. Comedians who are storytellers, not one-liners, they play with it to get the trivial details right. It affects the laugh you get. Whether the person you describe had a gray jacket or a blue jacket and had a golf swing like a bad barn door, or had a golf swing like a windy trip. That stuff matters. And they play with it and play with it until they get it the way it wants. So you can take a bass as a performer. You can take a bass joke out of a joke book. And you can tell it 22 different ways because of the trivial details that you put in. If it's got a dog in it, what breed of dog? And did the dog have one eye that pointed off in a funny direction? What was the dog's name? All that stuff affects the impact of the story. And if it's a joke, it impacts what kind of a laugh it gets. And if you listen to a long joke story, comedians who do not punchline, punchline, punchline, punchline, but long story punchline at end, you will find that the story is really detail rich all the way along. If you've never heard it, Google Charles Jarvis and try and find the bird joke. I used to be able to do it verbatim. I can't anymore. And Charlie still does a 10-tent. He's dead, but Charlie dead does a 10-tent better than I would do it. But it is one... Now, there's some laughs, but there's no punchlines along the way. And it is one long-ass joke to get to a punchline. And so Jarvis was like one of the number one humor speakers for a number of years in America. And his buddies all kept telling him he should go on Carson, and they finally got him on Carson, and it was horrid. And he bombed like probably nobody is it, because you only got like, you know, three minutes. And Charlie's used to using seven minutes to tell a joke. It's a different skill set, right? So if you go get the bird joke, you will see just how much rich detail there is all the way along. Plus it's a good joke. You'll enjoy it yourself. So specificity. I can almost always go through somebody's copy, and I can see that they are light on the specifics. Now, back to earlier, in many cases, they're light on specifics because they were overly concerned with length. So I may take four paragraphs to tell a story that could be told in a paragraph if you took out all the damn detail. If you left off, it was a dark and stormy night. If you left that out, obviously you could shorten the story. But now we're back to efficiency, effectiveness. That's really what we're back to. I Jack Eleven. This is a goodie. So what people, their preexisting belief is, if I only had X, everything would be different. So most small business owners, for example, believe if they could only get the capital they need, everything else would be different. Everything else would take care of itself. The reason I can't get anywhere is because no bank will loan me any money, and my mother-in-law won't give me any money, and I can't get the capital I need. That's their preexisting belief system. Almost everybody in a professional practice, dental, Cairo, podiatry, law, accounting, here's their preexisting belief. If we had twice as many new patients, everything else would be different. Of course, if everything else is royally screwed up, twice as many new patients will change very little, and it might even change things for the worse, not for the better. A miracle ever came when we started with a $2 million project, I begged them to take a million bucks off the table and go fix their phone and their salespeople. So just let's scale my thing in hand. Go take the money, fix your internal operations. Because if I pour four times as many prospects into the system you got, I'm going to actually make things worse. Because everything you're doing is screwed up. It's a mess. If Disney didn't function and you four times the number of people showing up at Disney every day, you wouldn't fix anything. Nothing fixes itself. I mean, it'd be a horror show. It's barely not that now. And they got everything running on all cylinders. So everybody has one of these beliefs. And it's there that then creates the lust for a one thing answer. And even smart people. So one of the five top questions I always get, got some version of it here yesterday, I think, right? Well, so it's an all different task in all different ways, but the point is the same. You just gave us 57 things, which are the five most important. But what he really wants to know is what's the one. Now, from a self-serving standpoint, see, you can't sell a card with one on it, even though that's what they want. Right? But beyond that, it's the wrong answer. But it's the answer everybody's looking for. It's why when I talked about the light your fire thing, yes, that's why it was so magical. Because I'm going to give you one thing to do. You're the only person who has to do it. He doesn't even have to know you're doing it. And magic will occur. Great. If he would just listen to me, this is going to fix that. This is going to do it. So people are very vulnerable to this, including smart people. So how many of you have read Gary Keller's book, The One Thing? Raise your hands. It's more instructive. Now look around at everybody. And all the suckers. Who should know better, you can put it down now. Who should know better before and after. And to be fair, the book is actually not about one thing. Right? It's the, you know, there's a mermaid inside. Come on in. You know, it's that. But in reality, you're all smart enough, you should have instantly rejected the premise. Just like the premise of the four hour work week. Right? You really should have refused to read it based on the title. But you couldn't wait to read it. Because even though you know better, you'd like there to be one thing. We'd all like there to be one thing. Right? So this lust for this is very high. And it makes people very vulnerable. So I can do an entire, as I have, three days, four days on money and wealth. Here's what I get mail about. The giving account, wealth account. That's what I get all the mail about. That's the fact. The one thing to go do. It's simple. It's easy. It does require a little discipline, but it's simple. It's easy. And it promises magic. And people go and say, this sounds like I gotta do the rest of this shit, but I'll do that thing. And it's magical. And that's what I get all the mail about. If that wasn't there, people kind of would be unhappy with the whole deal. They really like that. I know why they like it. Because it's one thing. It's simple and easy. And it's magical. Foster used to teach it. He used to teach it in a seven-day workshop. And he said, the only thing they really like is that. They tolerate all the rest of it, but they really like that. Of course. It's Viagra. One pill fixes everything. And so this is there. And you will see some version of the one thing in a lot of very smart market things. So I gave you, starting on page 63, I gave you some of the Burlson copy, where I have made the smile the one thing. If your kid has a perfect smile, everything else falls into place. All the negatives go away. There's no risk of them committing suicide, even though teenagers are committing suicide like every minute. There's no possibility of them being shamed and bullied in social media. So all the bad stuff goes away. And all the good stuff occurs. They'll have wonderful self-esteem. They'll get into a good college. They'll have a happy life. All will be well. One thing. That's all we got to do. You don't have to fix anything you're doing as a parent. You can be the same crappy parent you were yesterday. Just fix the kid's teeth and everything will be fine. That's what I've done. Now, maybe I should be ashamed, but I don't know. I don't. Then on page 67, so I've given you another example. This is a current control. And it is a, this is not direct mail. This is print. This is a magazine ad. So it's four pages of pretty dense copy. It runs in, so I'm starting on 67. It looks like this. It runs in high end, mostly, affluent oriented magazines. Robberport, Town and Country, private jet magazines that we only, people see if they fly private. There's a fake magazine put out for women in those terminals called New Beauty. I think you can also buy it at Bonson Noble, but it's not a real magazine. It's completely a sales document. But this is running in like elite traveler, and it's running in Town and Country for a product called Sellergy, which is Swiss cell therapy. Incidentally, the minute it's from somewhere else,
